item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I should also add that if you actually read the article in its entirety, you would know what all of that means. It’s common practice for articles to introduce words that seemingly do not make sense but are explained within the article.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: ok

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: this isn't remotely actionable or useful critique, and is a far better example of "using a lot of words to say nothing" than the skip it comments upon.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: i dont understand why something that exists in another reality is being classified as an SCP. TBH i couldnt be bothered to read through this entire massive wall of text, and the description alone gave me an internal sigh in the first sentence "located 3449 whalons to the Multiversal East of the Central Reality Compass and 87 whalons beyond where matter is ordinarily capable of permanently existing." what? was the point of this article to just string together as many words as possible, nevermind if anything you wrote makes any kind of sense at all? i got maybe 1/3 of the way down the page before i stopped. it just got worse with every sentence, like some guy was just sitting there with a thesaurus typing in whatever word he landed on at random. im sorry but if youre going to make up a bunch of fake concepts and objects, you need to put some effort into actually explaining them, otherwise its just literal nonsense.

item.title: I want to see so much more of this world
item.content:encoded:
Just give me a canon already, Tufto

Seriously though, amazing work. The writing is extremely evocative and poetic, and the atmosphere outstandingly amazing. Great work!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is a rare example of casual lore-dumping done in a way that doesn't feel lazy or boring. Very cool. It feels like the light around the lamp is metaphorical for the habitable zone of the multiverse. Both, though providing immense expanses in which to live, feel stiflingly small with the empty destructive void in sight. Those drawn to a settlement in the dark edge of the universe are of course the same drawn to the darkness around the lamp, because both of these darknesses are the same.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The actual written asides are evocative and engaging, but the worldbuilding relies too heavily on off-hand mentions of in-universe elements, such as artworks and locations and cultures, for me to visualize and become engaged in the world that the article wants to create. This leaves me feeling like the piece is bloated rather than expansive, which hinders my reception to the themes the piece tries to convey.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: cool

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
So take a car?

Just like… take a car, out to the whatever distance? You managed to get there on foot and send some radio waves back. And don't cars go more than twice as fast as a human can go on foot? So just like, take a car.

Better yet, holy shit, radio waves can traverse the nothingness? Send a drone. Just send a drone. With, like, a camera? On the drone? So it's never not sending pictures back? This is some real back-of-the-envelope fridge logic here. This wasn't hard to think of.

What's that? You say the technology is more susceptible than the human body to reality failure? Send a dog. Go ahead, just send a dog. Or better yet, a horse, trained to run in one direction upon command. You could fund that shit for like $5000. That's way cheaper than training a new human scientist from scratch. Probably more ethical, too.

I think that scientist got contaminated somehow from all the art majors.

item.title: Love this
item.content:encoded:
Here are my thoughts on it;

+ Show
- Hide

I'm deeply invested in philosophy relating to the known vs the unknown, as well as other similar concepts. Through reading on postmodernism and culture, I found myself to be repulsed by the fascination that contemporary philosophers give to the nature of unbeing, unreality, etc. I think 5005 is an appropriate allegory for humanity's general nature of fighting against the darkness, despite the intrigue of the unknown seeming far more appealing. I think that Ramirez' drifting into madness, plus the general disgust at the cultures and "ruralisms" of Lamplight, can be seen as someone going too far from humanity in order to understand the world (similar to postmodernism and the fates of many of their prominent philosophers) and in this case- meeting the abyss, and finding it to be unwelcoming of the human's curiosity. But what do I know?

The night does not give such easy answers.

- Hide

1+

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Hi, I loved this! Just chiming in, I noticed two typos that I'll list below:

"Lumiere down the street and into the fog, whistling the Blue Danube Waltz."

"writers and particularly academics who have been been resident in the town for less than a year."

Overall, I really enjoyed the worldbuilding and tone of this piece! Mahi loam, haha.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Incredible. Skips like these are a welcome break from the usual horror themed monsters that are usually written about (though I don't mind those either). The world building, characters, story, they're all extremely well written and appealing. I honestly wouldn't mind living here if it were a reality.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
No one needs me to say that this is crazy good, I just want to offer some other small interpretations that I got from this that I think add to the conversation.

first: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Marine_snow I think it's definitely extra-dimensional marine snow falling on a dead fish. "The snow gives rot." I don't have a clue what its existence and the fact that no one talks about it means for the story though.

The fact that it really is "just" an anglerfish maybe slaps a little humility into people who start thinking of Lamplight as a mythical place beyond human comprehension (which I like because it's a very Foundation mindset to believe that a place like this is understandable in the end). I also think it's really fitting that it's a giant anglerfish that draws artists in, and fits with the idea of learning more about the place and dying in the process.

I kind of feel like someone living in Lamplight when I'm reading this; I want to understand what it all means, but maybe it's just open to interpretation. My friend who's an artist is going to see things that are different than I, who was raised by university professors, do. Is it okay to enjoy this world without knowing the one 'right' meaning of everything? Is the real meaning the community that exists around stories like this? Or is there really a single correct interpretation of every detail that I'm just not seeing? Maybe if i reread it one more time, and do some more research, I don't need to sleep anyway…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I suppose I'm the minority here in that I'm not really a fan of this article.

I'll grant that it's an interesting concept, a society built in an alternate universe atop a dead fish. And there are several story elements that work well for an SCP article, such as the mysteries of the snow, the darkness, and the light. But what killed this for me was the execution. I found the article to be overly long, needlessly verbose, and too pretentious to take seriously.

Scraps of poetry?
Rambling discourses on the nihilism of life?
Sections of random word salad?

Judging by the comments a lot of folks here really like that kind of thing, which is fine. I just found it annoying. And, in my opinion, I would have thought it better to leave the true nature of the world vague to allow for speculation (though I admit it is a neat reveal).

It's clear the author worked hard on this and has some literary talent, but this didn't work for me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Wow, a romanticist work? Didn't think I'd ever see one on this sub. If you wanted to capture the thoughts and ideals of early 19th century poets,then you did an excellent job. People who were disappointed with the current state of their world but still unwilling to do anything to improve it,instead they tried to escape it. Some found refugee in nature,some in the glorious past of their nation's history and, apparently, some in an extradimensional settlement.
But as a medic, I still think they just suffered clinical depression from so much light deprivation.
You're probably not going to see this, but I'm happy to see some old school writing on this community, even though I'm personally not very interested in romaticism and hope to see more articles like this written in the near future.
+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I can imagine many more SCPs being made set in this multiversal future world, with so much set up for us as is.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'd have to disagree. My interpretation was that a major theme of the piece was embodied in how the people obsessed with the dark were missing the important things, both in that they ignored the community that the place was built to be, and in how they ignored the snow.

As such, I think the massive anti-climax of "oh yeah guess the anglerfish guy was right" is important for both bringing that message home to the reader - the mystery they were focused on unraveling turned out to be mundane - and for bringing attention to the fact that the *actual* mystery of the setting remains one - there's still that constant snow from nowhere.

The snow gives rot, complexity, ennui,
The night does not give such easy answers.

This stanza is the part of the SCP that I still struggle the most with, since it's incredibly contradictory, but I think the fact that what Ramirez discovers is an "easy answer" to the extent that someone had previously guessed it shows that the anglerfish isn't the answer.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I feel like actually mentioning an anglerfish earlier in the story makes it too obvious. Other than that it’s very good, though.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Yeah, this could be in The Scar.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Nice to see other Fallen London fans around.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I enjoyed this read and how everything tied into the reveal. It was fun visualizing the lamp light, the white orb, and the civilization surrounded by darkness. An outpost in the middle of literal nowhere.

The history and descriptions did seem a bit long, but that may be because I'm neither a historian, an artist, or a poet, as nearly everyone else seemed to love the worldbuilding.

Extremely minor typo (extra space):

beyond the limits of SCP-5005 -1's light range.

item.title: Re: Quick SPaG Check
item.content:encoded: Ooh good spot, will fix. Thanks :).

item.title: Quick SPaG Check
item.content:encoded:
Commenting because I absolutely adore this piece, and also because of a line I noticed in the final addendum:

"blood can be seen floating in from of the camera."

Think this should be fixed as "in front of the camera" or "in from off camera."

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Fuck. That was really fucking good. Like, upsettingly good. The worldbuilding, the poetry, the dialogue and characterization of Ramirez. The entire motif of a snow-covered city illuminated by lamplight, a cornucopia of syncretic future cultures… it's like, straight out of a fantasy piece, but it works so goddamn well. It's dreamlike. The entire thing is. It's describing a call to a very literal void, the one all creatives and artists get, trying to understand and unravel mysteries in your own head, against all odds — "the inevitable human sigh of defiance in the night." I'm having trouble articulating how perfect this is to me. It's fucking fantastic. Your best work yet, Tufts, and you know that's a lot coming from me.

item.title: Junior Staff Post
item.content:encoded: User SAFDEV, unless you have permission from the author to do so, please do not make edits that change or add content to the article. This page is not tagged collaborative.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I don't have anything to say that people haven't already said, but I'm going to say it anyway. The location is stellar. The worldbuilding (worldsbuilding, really) is stellar. That poem… I've already saved a personal copy of it. No objections of any kind to this work.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Is the true nature of lamplight a joke about the cynical interpretation of 4005? If so, I find that very appropriate for this article. Either way great entry. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Mahi means "strong" in Hawaiian, and when doubled up we get "mahi-mahi," or "very strong," which is the name for a fish. As a possible double pun, the mahi-mahi is known as "lampuka" in Maltese, and of course the whole thing is built under the Anglerfish's lamp. And loam is of course the intensely fertile top layer of soil created from the decay of various organisms - like the anglerfish decaying here. It might be coincidence but I think it's a reasonably subtle clue.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
"Behold its size. This is my canvas.
It's to be a cold, dark, and very gentle place.
…But first, I must see flame.
I wish to paint a picture.
Of a cold, dark, and very gentle place.
One day, it will make someone a goodly home.
That's why I must see flame."

This is amazing.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Why is this so good ? The ending just reflects the entire pointlessness so well.

A very good homage to modernism, spleen poetry, and the human nature. I know a few academics that would go nuts on this, excellent job :]

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: still my favorite of the 5000 entries, still easily worth the +1. Honestly I think this should've won but 5005 ain't a bad place to be

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I've never commented before, but I just had to say that this is easily one of my favorite pieces on the entire site. By far and away the 5000 entry that packs the biggest emotional punch. That last log is goddamn pitch-perfect. The worldbuilding is beautifully natural, the characters feel so real, and the descriptions of the environment paint a really incredibly vivid image. I first read this a couple weeks ago and I seriously cannot get it out of my head. This seriously accomplishes so much in only a few thousand words.

In addition, I think this fits very well for the contest. There's that beautiful moment that the mystery genre is so great for, where all of the hints click into place. Then, just enough is left unsaid for even the reader who did not figure out the solution ahead of time to have a moment of realization.

The only critical comment I might add is that turning the sections into collapsibles might make the article a bit easier to navigate.

I just really love this. Thank you for posting it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Incredible work as always Tufto! Far future Foundation stuff is very hit or miss for me, but this was an absolute home run. Loved the world building, the artistry, and the subversion to examining the costs of mysteries.

Also, I got big Fallen London/Sunless Skies vibes which is nice, as well as reminding me of the End of Time from Chrono Trigger.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Okay, THIS has my vote. It’s hard to say otherwise with how deeply this scip resonated with me. It’s so profound yet so mysertious. So beautiful yet so unearthly. Not to mention that the setting is fantastically built and executed. Wouldn’t mind an entire canon based on this, if people are willing to do so.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Madness inducing mystery vs misery inducing answers. Fantastic.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is just wonderful beyond words.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Stupendous. One of the best articles concerning art on the site.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I am in the proccess of reading House of Leaves and I think this is the best moment in my life to read this piece of art. Absolutely masterful.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Master fucking class. Absolute class, top lad. 100% gold here. I love it, love it. I can't say more than others have said, just that I love the atmosphere. You're one of the few writers that have transported me to the world you're writing, just the way you describe things, despite being a weird as fuck fantastical place, it feels 100% real man. It feels like you're there. I can only say that i wish I lived in lamplight, +1, I hope you do damn well in the contest.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Where did you come up with "mahi loam"? I don't know what it is, but something about that phrase is just so cool. It feels really good to say, too.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
In terms of writing, I really love it.

I just don't understand how so many people come to this place. The inhabitants I can wrap my head around but you mentioned travelers as well. How do people know this thing exists? It's beyond the edge of the multiverse, not to mention it's an SCP. Is it common knowledge in the future that you can travel between realities? There's no mention of it in the procedures.

And 'Contact your extrauniversal contact for more information' sounds a bit awkward. Is the second 'contact' a typo?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Fantastic work! Really good stuff, sadly i have nothing to say aside from what others have already said. +1

quick note, a couple spelling errors. the first instance of Kastomonu's name, and in the final log, "floating in from of the camera".

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Sorry for not upvoting it until now, but looks like you didn't need it.

This and 4005 show that prose and imagery is what you excel at. SCP-5000, even though this is the first we're hearing of it, instantly feels as real a place as canons with years of writing, such as Alagadda or Three Portlands. I especially love the little hints to some of your other work.

Feel very privileged to have critted this. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I've been complaining today about certain entries being too damn long. This is a case where the length is absolutely worth it. Superb world-building, not just for this deeply considered little piece of reality, but the future interdimensional Foundation that's exploring it.

Access is gained via a Scranton-Meyerbeer Arc from Site-Q46, located in the Aadzain Universe on the far side of the Western Cluster.

Where's that gif of Drew blinking in surprise when you need it? This is a good hook, it tells you things are about to get highly unreal.

Whalons. I love whalons. I want more of them.

But yeah, the more I read of this, the more I got the sense that the Foundation's response when asked to catalogue and define this anomaly was along the lines of, "Well, it's there, and otherwise we have no idea what the fuck is going on." I just love the image of a single, not very reliable lamp being literally the only light in the darkness, the only thing keeping reality together.

I also appreciate (what I gather is) the theme of art holding back nihilism. And that poem. And the final line, wow! Hearty +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is great but I’m disappointed in the fact it doesn’t reveal if the foundation told the populous of lamplight the answer and what they thought of it seeing as it’s pretty important to them. Another thing I don’t know what’s really supposed to be the point of this article if I don’t know if the reveal was intentionally disappointing or not, because both have different implications. It seems to be the former seeing as the anglerfish theory was one of the first and more mundane. A few errors in writing bothered me and more importantly I couldn’t proper how they were on the anglerfish because even though I assume they are on the side of the fish the lure is above them which doesn’t make much sense so it ends up being a bit confusing. Other then that good job. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: +1, masterpiece

item.title: that fallen londen feel
item.content:encoded: this feels very much like a sort of tomb colonies for the bohemian folk rather than the duelists you find infesting the standerd tomb colonies

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The whole mood of this is so dreamy and gentle. Really enjoyed the read.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I absolutely adore this. You could have used a few less proper nouns, but that’s a minor quibble.

You built a universe, explored the mystery contest theme twice-over, and you concluded with existential dread. The mood is perfect, the framing is great, and while it isn’t looking likely right now, this absolutely deserves the 5000 slot.

Well done.

(Also, I was immediately reminded of The End of Time from Chrono Trigger. Was that an inspiration, or just happy coincidence?)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Holy smokes, that is some top-notch worldbuilding.

Not only do you make the various denizens of Lamplight come alive, but your imagery of the city as a whole also provides a wonderful atmosphere for the whole thing to take place in.

While the end reveal as to the source of the light was a little underwhelming for me, the ride up to that point was easily worth the upvote.

+1 Tufto. Well done.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Just wanted to say that, so far, this is probably my favorite to win.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I want to be this good. Seriously, this makes me want to improve as a writer. Splendid work.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I absolutely fucking ADORE this. The worldbuilding that goes into it and the absolute bizarre nature of the setting are both genuinely incredible. However, something about the mystery element of it to me just feels lacking. Don't get me wrong, this is better than anything I've ever written in my life, but it just seems like the mystery itself is somewhat lackluster. It's a superb article, and I will definitely still upvote, but I think it might be better served as another 5000s skip.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I love it. Presentation fits perfectly with the story, progression of facts accompanied evolution of the writing itself. It start up brave, introducing all those terms and sudden revelation that humanity has reached beyond the veil of the universe. It starts confused, something that shouldn't by all account exist just stood there, in the middle of unfathomable night. As the story progresses the focus too, progresses, it starts with the existence of the place and the light, then the structure and the inside of the space, and then the outside. And it ended not with everything solved, but with something I want to interpret as solemn understanding and acceptance of what the Lamplight means. In its entirety, for everyone.

I'm probably rambling and overreaching. Eh.

I like Lumiere in particular. People came to Lamplight to see the night when the lamp and the city was brought and built by those seeking for a home. That resonated deeply with me. +1

item.title: Junior Staff - Site Crit
item.content:encoded:
[+ Opinion]
– hide block

The worldbuilding in this article is possibly the best on the entire site. Despite the frequent jargon, I somehow understood near-everything, with the few words I didn't fully understand feeling intentionally placed and beneficial to the overall narrative. This placed such a vivid image of the town in my head that I would've likely upvoted for that alone.

In addition to the incredible setting, the characters and narrative arc are pretty good as well. While initially I had reservations about the reveal being something that had already been mentioned earlier in the article, I eventually realised – I had no actual idea of what this thing was before the description, so all its prior mention did was help tie the article together and enhance the realism of the attached documents. It's pretty cool to see that prior theories were correct, and even cooler to see this without spoiling the imagery at the end.

The only aspect of this article that I wouldn't consider perfect is the choice of the term "expanse-material" in the description. While I can't think of a better term off the top of my head, both the hyphenation and the actual choice of words in the phrase seem a little off. This is clearly an extremely personal point though, and I'd never consider holding it against you if you stick with this phrasing.

Disclaimer: Opinions expressed in this review do not reflect the opinions of SCP Wiki staff as a whole. Please respond to gee0765 or contact the captain of the Site Criticism Team, SoullessSingularity, for any questions or concerns relating to this review.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The story, the worldbuilding, the characters. Masterful. The mystery encompassing it. I would recommend this even to everyone, and it is currently my favorite of all of the SCP-5000 submissions. It leaves you wanting more, in a good way. It has so many casual references to things that, as far as I am aware, didn't exist before this! The names of civilizations, the Orchard Earth, etc. I would absolutely love to know what a Harkhretian anglerfish is, or even what a Harkhretian is! If you go ahead and write anything more in this world, I would absolutely love to read it.
+1 so much.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I have to admit that my first go at reading this gave me a rather knee jerk reaction to not getting into the story due to the sheer amount of the jargon that is in this article. I didn't want to like how it felt like 1/3 of the SCP was overtly esoteric.

But I came back the next day to look at this again and realized that this one really stands out as an amazing example of the theme of the 5000 contest. The technical terminology does not take anything away, and really sets the foundation for a mysterious and haunting setting. As others have said this is something I could see working well for something like a Planescape or D&D world.

The narrative is interesting as well. It maybe takes a slight backseat to the world-building but I am okay with this. Easy upvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is wonderful. I daydream sometimes about what it’d be like if someone built a city in SCP-3001; couldn’t have asked for a fuller realization of that vision. I hope this wins and the 5000 series ends up filled with multiuniversal romance.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
There's this spelling mistake I spotted, in the third section titled Culture, third paragraph second line. It should be "painters" instead of "panters" right?

In general this was really impressive. You put a lot of effort in world-building, and I think it paid off. And as an art enthusiast, the part where you talked about architecture and visual arts really resonated.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Definitely my favorite of the 5000 entries at the moment. As everyone has said the worldbuilding is exceptional and there's just such a somber atmosphere through it all.

It also struck the perfect balance of technical terms. Many articles overexplain or underexplain terms but this nailed it perfectly.

So far it looks like this one won't win which is a shame (I know it's super early and it is rated well but it's a tier below the top). I think having a X000 entry like this would be very different and unique from the others.
